title: ICE Access and Immigration-Enforcement Coordination Records — Industry Station / Hacienda Heights (TRUTH Act)

communications.subject: California Public Records Act Request: ICE Access and Immigration-Enforcement Coordination Records — Industry Station / Hacienda Heights (TRUTH Act)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the California Public Records Act, I hereby request the following records:

This request concerns records that the Transparent Review of Unjust Transfers and Holds Act (the TRUTH Act, Assembly Bill 2792, Government Code sections 7283 and following) expressly designates as public records subject to the California Public Records Act, namely all records relating to access provided by the Los Angeles County Sheriff's Department to United States Immigration and Customs Enforcement, including all communications with that agency concerning such access. The request also concerns the Department's compliance with the California Values Act (Senate Bill 54, Government Code sections 7284 and following). I am directing this request to records associated with the Industry Station and its patrol responsibility for the unincorporated community of Hacienda Heights, and I am requesting records covering the period from January 1, 2025 through the date this request is processed.

I request, first, all records of ICE access to any individual in the Department's custody, as the term access is used in the TRUTH Act, including access logs, the written consent forms provided to individuals before any interview, and any records documenting whether such access was granted or declined. Second, I request copies of all immigration detainers, notification requests, transfer requests, administrative warrants, and Forms I-247, I-200, and I-205 received from Immigration and Customs Enforcement or United States Customs and Border Protection, together with the Department's response to each. Third, I request all communications, including emails, letters, memoranda, text messages, and call or visit logs, between Department personnel and Immigration and Customs Enforcement, Customs and Border Protection, or the Department of Homeland Security concerning access to, notification about, or the transfer of any individual.

Fourth, I request all policies, directives, training bulletins, unit orders, and any memoranda of understanding or written agreements governing the Department's interaction with federal immigration authorities, including any provisions that address the use of private contractors to carry out transfers of individuals to federal custody. Fifth, I request all records reflecting Department personnel participation in, or support of, any federal immigration enforcement operation conducted within the Industry Station service area, including records of perimeter security, traffic control, scene assistance, or any officer-safety assist function provided in connection with such an operation. Sixth, I request all data, reports, and presentation materials that the Department prepared for or provided to the Los Angeles County Board of Supervisors in connection with any community forum held under the TRUTH Act, along with records identifying the dates on which such forums were held.

I understand that personal identifying information may be redacted before disclosure, and I am not seeking that information; a permissible redaction does not justify withholding an otherwise responsive record. If any responsive record is withheld in whole or in part, please identify the record and state the specific exemption claimed, and please note that the TRUTH Act independently designates records relating to ICE access as public. I would appreciate rolling production of records as they are located rather than a single delayed release, and I am willing to narrow or clarify any category upon request.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 10 calendar days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

John Avila

In the November 20, 2025 memo written by Conrad Timbers, Acting Chief Portfolio Officer, he states on page 4, "Moreover, their class size compliance review found that ten of forty-two K–8 classrooms are currently out of alignment with the Illinois State Board of Education (ISBE) 70/30 rule. OSD’s report recommends that the master schedule is revisited to ensure efficient use of instructional time and staffing and immediately addressing the issues with the ISBE 70/30 rule."

Pursuant to the Massachusetts Public Records Law, I hereby request the following records:

Subject: Public Records Request: Emergency Medical Records & CAD Logs - Incident #110004917

To the Boston EMS Public Records Officer,

Pursuant to the Massachusetts Public Records Law (M.G.L. c. 66, § 10), I hereby request access to all records maintained by Boston EMS regarding the emergency response to 141 W. Newton St, Boston, MA, on January 4, 2011, associated with Boston Police Incident Report #110004917.

Specifically, I am requesting:

1. All Computer-Aided Dispatch (CAD) logs or equivalent dispatch records generated by Boston EMS for this incident.

2. Any and all Emergency Medical Service (EMS) run reports, patient care reports (PCRs), or incident summary sheets generated by the responding EMS personnel at this location and time.

3. Any audio logs of dispatch communications regarding this incident.

Context: I have already filed a request with the Boston Police Department (BPD) regarding this incident, and they indicated they have no responsive records and directed me to Boston EMS. As Boston EMS is the primary agency for medical dispatch and response, these records should be within your custody and control.

If any portion of these records is deemed exempt under HIPAA or other statutes, please redact only the specific protected health information (PHI) and provide the remainder of the records as required by the Massachusetts Public Records Law.

I also request that, if appropriate, fees be waived as we believe this request is in the public interest, as suggested but not stipulated by the recommendations of the Massachusetts Supervisor of Public Records. The requested documents will be made available to the general public free of charge as part of the public information service at MuckRock.com, and is not made for commercial usage.

I expect the request to be filled in an accessible format, including for screen readers, which provide text-to-speech for persons unable to read print. Files that are not accessible to screen readers include, for example, .pdf image files as well as physical documents.

title: KING COUNTY DIRECTORS ASSOCIATION – Disbursements, Employee Expenses, and Payroll/HR Records 2022-2026

communications.communication:
KING COUNTY DIRECTORS ASSOCIATION – Disbursements, Employee Expenses, and Payroll/HR Records 2022-2026

To the KCDA Public Records Officer:

Pursuant to the Washington State Public Records Act (RCW 42.56), I am requesting copies of the following identifiable public records that were created, received, or maintained in the care, custody, or control of the King County Directors' Association (KCDA) between January 1, 2022, and the present.

Please produce the complete, unredacted records for the following categories. Where applicable, this request specifically targets the records of KCDA Executive Director David Malhalko, Yohan Lee, Kristen Deskin, and any KCDA Board Members, but applies broadly to all KCDA expenditures as defined below:

1. Disbursements and Employee Expenses

 Credit Card (P-Card) Records: All itemized monthly account statements for all KCDA-issued credit cards, purchasing cards (P-Cards), or corporate charge accounts. This must include all individual corresponding receipts, point-of-sale invoices, and internal authorization/approval signatures for each charge.
 Travel and Expense Vouchers: All employee and executive travel pre-authorization forms, post-travel expense reports, mileage tracking logs, hotel folios, airline booking receipts, conference registration receipts, and per diem disbursement records.
 Invoices and Check Registers: The complete internal check register (or digital equivalent) detailing all checks cut or EFTs/wire transfers issued for employee reimbursements. Please include the underlying invoices, expense claims, or proof of purchase that justified each disbursement.

2. Executive Compensation and Bonus Structures

 Executive Contracts: The complete, fully executed employment contracts and subsequent amendments for KCDA executive staff (specifically including, but not limited to, David Malhalko and Yohan Lee).
 Bonus and Incentive Documentation: All records, memos, rubrics, and formulas detailing any performance bonuses, incentive pay, or variable compensation tied to KCDA’s collection of vendor sales, rebates, or administrative fees.
 Board Authorizations: All official Board of Directors resolutions, meeting minutes, or executive session summaries that formally approved, authorized, or altered the compensation, raises, or bonuses of KCDA executive staff.

3. Payroll and Human Resources

 Payroll Registers: Comprehensive payroll registers or master payroll summaries for KCDA executive and administrative staff, detailing gross pay, net pay, bonuses, stipends, benefits contributions, and deductions per pay period.
 Timesheets and Leave Records: All formal timesheets, clock-in/clock-out logs, or digital attendance records providing proof of hours actually worked by KCDA employees. Furthermore, please produce all tracking records for accrued, used, and cashed-out vacation, sick, or administrative leave.

RECORDS INSTALLMENTS

If production of these email records will be completed in multiple installments, please begin by producing responsive email records in REVERSE chronological order by date, producing the most recent messages and records first.

SEARCH FOR RESPONSIVE RECORDS

Your agency’s search for responsive email records should involve a good-faith search of all public employee personal devices reasonably believed to contain requested responsive records regardless the type of device or account from which the communication was generated or where the device or data is stored (including personal devices such as personal cell phones, laptops, tablets, and/or computers etc. used from home to conduct public business during the COVID-19 Pandemic). Nissen v. Pierce County, 183 Wa.2d 863 (2015).

Also, please provide a reasonably detailed, nonconclusory affidavit attesting to the nature and extent of any search of any privately owned public employee computer, device, or account.

SCOPE AND FORMAT

This request includes documents maintained natively on KCDA servers, physical HR/Accounting files, and data hosted within third-party financial or payroll software (e.g., ADP, QuickBooks, ERP systems) utilized by KCDA. Please provide these records in their native electronic format (such as Excel .xlsx files for ledgers) or as searchable PDFs.

If you claim any portion of these financial or personnel records is exempt from disclosure (such as specific bank routing numbers or personal social security numbers), you must redact only the specific exempt information and produce the remainder of the document. You must also provide a detailed exemption log citing the specific statutory basis for every redaction, as required by RCW 42.56.210(3).

EXEMPTION LOGS

Please ensure any agency redactions or exemptions claimed in the production of responsive records are accompanied by a complete and detailed exemption log noting the valid legal reason for each exemption at each redaction location in the record as well as the specific number of pages if any that your agency redacts or withholds in their entirety.

FEE ESTIMATE AND CONSOLIDATION

Pursuant to the Washington State Public Records Act mandate that agencies provide the 'fullest assistance to inquirers' (RCW 42.56.100) and utilize the 'most reasonable cost-efficient method available' for electronic records (RCW 42.56.120), KCDA must consolidate all responsive electronic records prior to generating any fee estimate. KCDA is expressly instructed not to artificially inflate production costs by 'unpacking,' extracting, or converting native bulk files (such as .pst email databases) into thousands of individual .pdf or .msg files in an attempt to exploit the statutory per-page or per-file fee structures. If individual electronic files or attachments are gathered, I require that KCDA consolidate them into a single compressed .zip file or a single PDF Portfolio *before* calculating the cost of production. Generating a fee estimate based on an artificially expanded file count or unnecessary format conversion—rather than utilizing the highly cost-efficient 10-cents-per-gigabyte native transmission rate or a consolidated single-file rate—constitutes an unreasonable barrier to public access and a direct violation of KCDA’s statutory duties.

PRIOR DESTRUCTION OF RECORDS

If any potentially relevant records have been destroyed, please provide copies of the destruction or transfer slips as well as any other documentation pertaining to, mentioning, or describing said transfer or destruction, including, but not limited to, a statement that your agency has no additional copies of the records in question.
Please ensure any agency redactions or exemptions claimed in the production of responsive records are accompanied by a complete and detailed exemption log noting the valid legal reason for each exemption at each redaction location in the record as well as the specific number of pages if any that your agency redacts or withholds in their entirety.

This request is not commercial in nature and does not seek a list of any kind in any or in any form.

title: LONGVIEW SCHOOL DISTRICT – Felony Arrest of Longview School District Superintendent Karen Cloninger  and Mark Morris High School Sexual Assault Investigation. (Longview School District)

communications.communication:
Via email: publicrecordsrequest@longviewschools.org

LONGVIEW SCHOOL DISTRICT – Felony Arrest of Longview School District Superintendent Karen Cloninger  and Mark Morris High School Sexual Assault Investigation.
CC: WASHINGTON OFFICE OF SUPERINTENDENT OF PUBLIC INSTRUCTION
CC: SPOKANE PUBLIC SCHOOLS DISTRICT

Oregon Public Broadcasting: Longview superintendent arrested on obstruction charges for high school sexual assault investigation
https://www.opb.org/article/2026/05/21/longview-high-school-superintendent-arrested-on-obstruction-charges-for-sexual-assault-investigation/

KGW8: Court documents detail why Longview School District's superintendent is charged in an alleged cover-up of abuse
https://www.kgw.com/article/news/education/longview-school-district-superintdent-karen-cloninger-arrest-sex-assault-court-docs/283-a1d5d93e-ad17-4ded-87cf-e956448e3d7b

KOIN: ‘Don’t talk about this’: Longview school superintendent allegedly told staff not to report abuse
https://www.koin.com/news/washington/dont-talk-about-this-longview-school-superintendent-allegedly-told-staff-not-to-report-abuse/

To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Washington Public Records Act [RCW 42.56] I hereby request the following records:

1. A complete copy of any and all records and writings regarding the investigation and subsequent arrest of Longview School District Superintendent Karen Cloninger.
2. The complete .pst file(s) of any and all email accounts assigned to School District Superintendent Karen Cloninger in your agency possession.
3. The complete personnel file of School District Superintendent Karen Cloninger. 

Responsive records include but are not limited to:

Law Enforcement reports and communications
Parent and Student communications and notifications
Incident reports, incident analysis, emails, incident follow up
Media communications, press releases
Court records, witness statements, etc. 
All insurance records 
Copies of Investigative Reports
Legal records, legal emails, demand letters, etc.
Complaints and communications referencing assault
Copies of all public records requests for records concerning or relating to sexual assault at Morris High School and/or Superintendent Karen Cloninger.
All Communications of Superintendent Karen Cloninger January 1, 2024 - Present.
Email (including headers, message bodies, inline images, and attachments).
Text/SMS/MMS (including iMessage, Android, carrier SMS).
Instant messaging & collaboration: Microsoft Teams/Skype, Slack, Google Chat, Zoom/Zoom Chat, Webex, RingCentral, WhatsApp, Signal, Telegram, etc.
Calendars/Invites (meeting requests, agendas, minutes, chat during meetings).
Voicemail logs/transcripts/recordings and call logs (if used for vendor contact).
File shares & cloud drives that contain such communications (SharePoint/OneDrive/Google Drive/Box/Dropbox).
Letters, memos, meeting notes, handouts, and presentations
Related records about those communications
Contracts/agreements, SOWs, amendments, quotes, order forms, renewals, and NDAs.
Invoices, purchase orders, payment records, and W-9s.
Litigation hold(s) or preservation notices relating to these communications.

DEFINITION OF RECORDS AND WRITINGS

RECORDS AND WRITINGS as defined by RCW 42.56.010(4): any handwriting, typewriting, printing, photostating, photographing, and every other means of recording any form of communication or representation including, but not limited to, letters, words, pictures, sounds, or symbols, or combination thereof, and all papers, maps, magnetic or paper tapes, photographic films and prints, motion picture, film and video recordings, magnetic or punched cards, discs, drums, diskettes, sound recordings, and other documents including existing data compilations from which information may be obtained or translated.

PRODUCTION PROCESSING AND DELIVERY OF ELECTRONIC RECORDS

Please provide all records in their native electronic format. Please do not instead create new records by printing electronic files then scanning and printing the newly scanned prints.

To preserve metadata and ease review, please produce as follows:

Emails: PST ( Microsoft Outlook ) or MBOX containers; if unavailable, EML/MSG with full headers.
Chats/Texts/IM: native exports where supported; otherwise, CSV/JSON with fields for sender, recipients, timestamps (UTC), message/thread IDs, and attachments (export attachments in original formats).
Calendars: ICS where possible; or PDF plus any attachments.
Documents/attachments: original/native formats (DOCX, XLSX, PPTX, PDF, etc.).
Logs/Reports: CSV/JSON preferred.
Delivery: Direct electronic transmission via email attachments or open (no login/registration/password) download link hosted by your agency (e.g., SharePoint/OneDrive public link). Please do not use third-party portals that require creating an account or logging in. A single or series of .zip files is fine.

If any records cannot be produced in the above formats, please produce them in their native format with associated metadata intact.

METADATA REQUESTED

Where reasonably available, please include customary metadata fields such as: From, To, CC, BCC, Subject, Sent/Received Date-Time (with timezone/UTC), Message-ID, Conversation/Thread-ID, file names, file sizes, hash values (if held), and folder/location paths.

SEARCH DETAILS AND RECORDS PRESERVATION

Please identify the systems searched e.g.  (Exchange/Outlook/Office 365, Teams, mobile devices, MDM archives, JustFOIA/CivicPlus admin, helpdesk, file shares) and the custodians.

Please describe the search methods/terms used.

If any potentially responsive sources were not searched or are no longer available, please explain why and what preservation steps were taken.

USE OF THIRD PARTY PORTALS NOT MANDATORY

Please note that under Washington’s Public Records Act, use of a third-party “portal” is not mandatory for requestors. Agencies may not condition access to records on the creation of an account, the use of a login, or the acceptance of third-party vendor terms.

Third-party portals (such as GovQA, NextRequest, JustFOIA, or similar systems) often:

Require account creation or login credentials;

Restrict downloads, viewing times, or re-access;

Remove or disable links after a limited period;

Place records behind commercial platforms operated by for-profit corporations.

These restrictions unlawfully burden and condition access to public records. The Public Records Act forbids agencies from imposing access barriers beyond those authorized by statute.

RCW 42.56.080(2): An agency “shall not deny a request for identifiable public records solely on the basis that the request is overbroad.” Agencies must provide the “fullest assistance” and “most timely possible action” to requestors.

RCW 42.56.550(1): Courts are directed to liberally construe the PRA to ensure access and strictly construe exemptions against withholding.

Fisher Broadcasting-Seattle TV LLC v. City of Seattle, 180 Wn.2d 515, 528 (2014): Agencies must produce records in a “reasonably usable format when feasible.”

Mechling v. City of Monroe, 152 Wn. App. 830, 841 (2009): PRA is to be liberally construed in favor of disclosure and cannot be restricted by agency practices.

Because of this, I respectfully decline use of any third-party portal. Please provide all responsive records via:

Direct electronic email attachment to this requestor’s address, or

A direct, open (no password, no registration required) cloud-based download link hosted by your agency.

Do not register this requestor with, nor direct this requestor to, any third-party commercial portal, including but not limited to FOIAOnline, GovQA (a Granicus Corporation), NextRequest, FOIAExpress, JustFOIA, FOIADirect, or any similar online service.

Do not transmit any portion of this request, or responsive records, to any for-profit vendor or portal. Doing so would unlawfully condition access to public records and contravene both RCW 42.56.080 and RCW 42.56.550.

PUBLIC RECORDS REQUESTS SUBMITTED VIA EMAIL

This public records request is being submitted via email, consistent with Washington’s Public Records Act.

RCW 42.56.080(2) provides in relevant part:

“Agencies shall honor requests received in person during an agency’s normal office hours, or by mail or email, for identifiable public records unless exempted by provisions of this chapter. No official format is required for making a records request; however, agencies may recommend that requestors submit requests using an agency-provided form or web page.”

Accordingly:

A request submitted by email is a valid, enforceable method of making a PRA request.

Agencies may recommend, but cannot require, the use of an online portal or official form.

Any refusal to honor this request as submitted would directly conflict with RCW 42.56.080 and the agency’s duty under RCW 42.56.100 to provide the “fullest assistance to requestors” and adopt reasonable rules that do not “unreasonably hinder or delay” access.

Therefore, this request is to be honored and fulfilled by your agency via email, as submitted. Should the agency fail to process this request in compliance with statute, relief will be sought pursuant to RCW 42.56.550 in the form of judicial review, penalties, and attorney’s fees.

INSTALLMENTS AND FEE ESTIMATES

Please provide:

An initial acknowledgment within five business days (RCW 42.56.520) with a reasonable time estimate for each installment, and

A cost estimate if fees are anticipated (please itemize and explain).

NISSEN AFFIDAVIT AND ALL DEVICE SEARCH REQUIRED

Pursuant to Nissen v. Pierce County, 183 Wn.2d 863 (2015), and its progeny, this request specifically includes all responsive public records contained on personal devices or personal accounts (including personal cell phones, text/SMS/MMS, personal email accounts, cloud storage, instant messaging apps, and social media accounts) of any agency employee, official, or contractor where public business may have been conducted.

To comply with this requirement, please:

Conduct a comprehensive all-device search, including both agency-issued and personally owned devices/accounts used for public business.

Require each potentially responsive custodian to execute a Nissen affidavit or sworn certification affirming:

That they conducted a diligent search of all personal and agency devices/accounts reasonably likely to contain responsive records;

The methods used to conduct the search (e.g., search terms, accounts checked, device types); and

Whether any responsive records were located, preserved, and produced.

Produce all responsive records located in such searches along with the executed affidavits.

Failure to obtain and provide sworn Nissen affidavits or equivalent certifications from custodians may constitute an incomplete search in violation of RCW 42.56.520 (requirement for a reasonable and adequate search) and may expose the agency to relief under RCW 42.56.550.

EXEMPTION LOGS

Please ensure that all redactions or exemptions claimed by your agency in the production of responsive records are accompanied by a complete and detailed exemption log.

The log must:

* Identify the valid legal reason (statutory citation) for each exemption;
* Specify the location of each redaction within the record produced (via footnote, coded reference, or margin notation);
* State the number of pages or records withheld in their entirety, if any; and
* Demonstrate that only the **minimal exempt portion** of any record has been withheld, as required by law.

The Washington Public Records Act requires agencies to justify each withholding or redaction:

RCW 42.56.210(3): “Agency responses refusing, in whole or in part, inspection of any public record shall include a statement of the specific exemption authorizing the withholding of the record (or part) and a brief explanation of how the exemption applies to the record withheld.”
RCW 42.56.210(1): Exemptions must be narrowly construed.

Washington courts have repeatedly enforced this duty:

Rental Housing Ass’n of Puget Sound v. City of Des Moines, 165 Wn.2d 525, 538 (2009): Agencies must provide specific explanations for each exemption; conclusory or vague justifications are insufficient.
Sanders v. State*, 169 Wn.2d 827, 845 (2010): “The burden of proof is on the agency to establish that refusal to permit public inspection and copying is in accordance with a statute.”
Resident Action Council v. Seattle Housing Auth.* 177 Wn.2d 417, 433 (2013): Agencies must disclose the non-exempt portions*of records and cannot withhold more than necessary.

Therefore, I request that your agency provide an itemized exemption log with each installment of records produced, in compliance with RCW 42.56.210(3) and the above case law, ensuring transparency and accountability in the redaction process.

COMMERCIAL PURPOSE DECLARATION

Commercial Purpose Declaration

This public records request is not being made for any commercial purpose. The Washington State Public Records Act (PRA) prohibits agencies from disclosing “lists of individuals” for commercial purposes.

RCW 42.56.070(8) states:

“This chapter shall not be construed as giving authority to any agency to give, sell, or provide access to lists of individuals requested for commercial purposes….”

Accordingly:

This request does not seek a “list of individuals.”

This request is not made for any commercial purpose.

This declaration satisfies all requirements of RCW 42.56.070(8) regarding prohibitions on lists and commercial purposes.

Warning: The PRA does not authorize agencies to require requestors to complete additional “commercial purpose declarations” when the request does not seek a list of individuals. Such requirements improperly condition access to public records in violation of RCW 42.56.080(2) (requests must be honored by mail or email, no official format required) and RCW 42.56.100 (agencies must provide the fullest assistance to requestors and may not adopt rules that unreasonably hinder or delay access).

Washington courts have consistently rejected agency attempts to impose conditions not found in statute:

Mechling v. City of Monroe, 152 Wn. App. 830, 841 (2009): The PRA is a strongly worded mandate for broad disclosure; agencies cannot erect unauthorized barriers.

SEIU 925 v. University of Washington, 193 Wn.2d 860, 875 (2019): The PRA is to be liberally construed to promote access, and exemptions must be narrowly applied.

Therefore, no additional commercial purpose declaration will be completed for this request. Any attempt to impose such a requirement would be an unlawful restriction of rights under RCW 42.56.

PRESERVATION OF PUBLIC RECORDS *** ANTICIPATE LITIGATION ***

WAC 44-14-03005 RETENTION OF PUBLIC RECORDS

WAC 44-14-03005 (Retention of Public Records) makes clear that public records are subject to retention schedules, and that unlawful destruction can constitute a crime.

RCW 40.16.010 and RCW 40.16.020 criminalize the unlawful mutilation, destruction, or concealment of public records.

RCW 42.56.100 expressly prohibits an agency from destroying any public record after a public records request has been made for that record, even if the record would otherwise be scheduled for lawful destruction.

In addition, agencies have heightened retention obligations when litigation is reasonably anticipated. Records potentially relevant to actual or anticipated litigation must be preserved until both the public records request and the litigation have been fully resolved.

Legal Consequences of Premature Destruction

In Yacobellis v. City of Bellingham, 55 Wn. App. 706, 780 P.2d 272 (1989), the Court of Appeals held that an agency violates the Public Records Act and is subject to attorneys’ fees and statutory penalties when it destroys responsive records after receiving a public records request.

By contrast, it is not a PRA violation if a record was destroyed prior to the agency’s receipt of a request, provided the destruction was lawful under retention schedules. See Bldg. Indus. Ass’n of Wash. v. McCarthy, 152 Wn. App. 720, 218 P.3d 196 (2009); West v. Dep’t of Natural Resources, 163 Wn. App. 238, 258 P.3d 78 (2011).

Washington courts have emphasized that the Public Records Act (RCW 42.56) and the state’s records retention laws (RCW 40.14) are separate but complementary. Agencies must comply with both.

Accordingly:

Your agency must preserve all potentially responsive records immediately upon receipt of this request.

No responsive records may be altered, deleted, or destroyed until this request has been fully resolved and any related litigation has been lifted.

Any unlawful destruction of responsive records may expose the agency and its officials to criminal liability (RCW 40.16), civil penalties, and mandatory attorneys’ fees (RCW 42.56.550(4)).

RECORDS INSTALLMENTS - ORDER OF PRODUCTION

If production of responsive records will be completed in multiple installments, production in reverse chronological order is strongly preferred to the extent supported by native system exports, provided it does not delay the delivery of installments.

DIGITAL COMPRESSION AND FILE MINIMIZATION REQUESTED IN ACCORDANCE WITH RCW 42.56

Pursuant to RCW 42.56.080(2), please provide all responsive electronic records in the fewest number of individual files, preserving native formats wherever possible. This public records request is not being made for any commercial purpose. The Washington State Public Records Act (PRA) prohibits agencies from disclosing “lists of individuals” for commercial purposes.

RCW 42.56.070(8) states:

“This chapter shall not be construed as giving authority to any agency to give, sell, or provide access to lists of individuals requested for commercial purposes….”

Accordingly:

This request does not seek a “list of individuals.”

This request is not made for any commercial purpose.

This declaration satisfies all requirements of RCW 42.56.070(8) regarding prohibitions on lists and commercial purposes.

Warning: The PRA does not authorize agencies to require requestors to complete additional “commercial purpose declarations” when the request does not seek a list of individuals. Such requirements improperly condition access to public records in violation of RCW 42.56.080(2) (requests must be honored by mail or email, no official format required) and RCW 42.56.100 (agencies must provide the fullest assistance to requestors and may not adopt rules that unreasonably hinder or delay access).

Washington courts have consistently rejected agency attempts to impose conditions not found in statute:

Mechling v. City of Monroe, 152 Wn. App. 830, 841 (2009): The PRA is a strongly worded mandate for broad disclosure; agencies cannot erect unauthorized barriers.

SEIU 925 v. University of Washington, 193 Wn.2d 860, 875 (2019): The PRA is to be liberally construed to promote access, and exemptions must be narrowly applied.

Therefore, no additional commercial purpose declaration will be completed for this request. Any attempt to impose such a requirement would be an unlawful restriction of rights under RCW 42.56.

PRESERVATION OF PUBLIC RECORDS *** ANTICIPATE LITIGATION ***

WAC 44-14-03005 RETENTION OF PUBLIC RECORDS

WAC 44-14-03005 (Retention of Public Records) makes clear that public records are subject to retention schedules, and that unlawful destruction can constitute a crime.

RCW 40.16.010 and RCW 40.16.020 criminalize the unlawful mutilation, destruction, or concealment of public records.

RCW 42.56.100 expressly prohibits an agency from destroying any public record after a public records request has been made for that record, even if the record would otherwise be scheduled for lawful destruction.

In addition, agencies have heightened retention obligations when litigation is reasonably anticipated. Records potentially relevant to actual or anticipated litigation must be preserved until both the public records request and the litigation have been fully resolved.

Legal Consequences of Premature Destruction

In Yacobellis v. City of Bellingham, 55 Wn. App. 706, 780 P.2d 272 (1989), the Court of Appeals held that an agency violates the Public Records Act and is subject to attorneys’ fees and statutory penalties when it destroys responsive records after receiving a public records request.

By contrast, it is not a PRA violation if a record was destroyed prior to the agency’s receipt of a request, provided the destruction was lawful under retention schedules. See Bldg. Indus. Ass’n of Wash. v. McCarthy, 152 Wn. App. 720, 218 P.3d 196 (2009); West v. Dep’t of Natural Resources, 163 Wn. App. 238, 258 P.3d 78 (2011).

Washington courts have emphasized that the Public Records Act (RCW 42.56) and the state’s records retention laws (RCW 40.14) are separate but complementary. Agencies must comply with both.

Accordingly:

Your agency must preserve all potentially responsive records immediately upon receipt of this request.

No responsive records may be altered, deleted, or destroyed until this request has been fully resolved and any related litigation has been lifted.

Any unlawful destruction of responsive records may expose the agency and its officials to criminal liability (RCW 40.16), civil penalties, and mandatory attorneys’ fees (RCW 42.56.550(4)).

RECORDS INSTALLMENTS - ORDER OF PRODUCTION

If production of responsive records will be completed in multiple installments, production in reverse chronological order is strongly preferred to the extent supported by native system exports, provided it does not delay the delivery of installments.

DIGITAL COMPRESSION AND FILE MINIMIZATION REQUESTED IN ACCORDANCE WITH RCW 42.56

Pursuant to RCW 42.56.080(2), please provide all responsive electronic records in the fewest number of individual files, preserving native formats wherever possible. Where appropriate, please use free digital compression (.zip or equivalent) to consolidate responsive records into the smallest number of installments for efficiency and economy.

This approach reduces agency burden, promotes prompt disclosure, and minimizes potential costs to the requestor, consistent with the Public Records Act’s directive that agencies provide records in the most “reasonably locatable form” and “fullest assistance to requestors.” See RCW 42.56.100 (agency duty to adopt reasonable rules for providing fullest assistance); RCW 42.56.120(2)(b) (fees must reflect actual costs, not used to discourage access).

Washington courts have consistently held that agencies must facilitate access in a practical, non-burdensome manner. See Fisher Broadcasting-Seattle TV LLC v. City of Seattle, 180 Wn.2d 515, 528 (2014) (agencies must provide records in a usable format when reasonably feasible); Mechling v. City of Monroe, 152 Wn. App. 830, 841 (2009) (PRA is to be liberally construed in favor of disclosure). Producing consolidated, digitally compressed files satisfies these obligations by reducing unnecessary fragmentation and excess per-page charges.

Accordingly, I request that each installment be produced as a single compressed .zip file (or as few as technically necessary), containing all responsive records in native format, to ensure both compliance with RCW 42.56 and an economical calculation of any applicable public records fees.

COMMUNICATION VIA EMAIL

Please send all requests for clarification, status updates, and production of responsive electronic documents via direct email reply.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. We look forward to receiving your response to this request within 5 business days, as the statute requires.

Good Luck.

title: FOIA Request: Privately Funded Water Filtration System at City-Owned Facility

communications.subject: Michigan Freedom of Information Act Request: FOIA Request: Privately Funded Water Filtration System at City-Owned Facility
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Michigan Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

This request is directed to the attention of Michelle Dewey, FOIA Coordinator, City of Adrian, in accordance with the City’s published FOIA Procedures and Guidelines.

I request copies of public records related to the privately funded water filtration system referenced in the City of Adrian’s May 22, 2026 public statement, which stated in part: “A water filtration system was installed recently at a City owned facility (not City Hall). The system was not paid for by the City or taxpayer dollars, it was privately paid for.”

I request the following records:

1. Any record identifying the City-owned facility where the water filtration system was installed, including the facility name, address, or department served. This includes any installation work order, facilities request, maintenance log, building maintenance record, plumbing permit, building permit, internal memo, or similar record identifying the location.

2. Any record identifying the individual, company, foundation, nonprofit, vendor, or other entity that donated, paid for, reimbursed, or otherwise funded the installation or purchase of the water filtration system. This includes any donation acknowledgment, gift letter, thank-you correspondence, invoice, receipt, proof of payment, reimbursement record, City Commission agenda item, consent agenda item, or internal approval record.

3. Any record identifying the make, model, manufacturer, capacity, filter media, treatment specifications, maintenance requirements, and contaminants or compounds the system is designed to remove. This includes any specification sheet, manufacturer brochure, installation manual, warranty document, vendor quote, invoice, or product literature.

4. Any agreement, authorization, approval, memo, correspondence, or internal communication from January 1, 2025 through May 31, 2026 concerning the acceptance, installation, funding, donation, ownership, maintenance, replacement filters, or use of the privately funded water filtration system referenced in the May 22, 2026 public statement.

Please include complete copies of responsive records, including all pages, attachments, enclosures, transmittal emails, routing records, and related packet materials where applicable. Where records exist electronically, I request that they be provided in their existing electronic format where reasonably available.

I request a waiver or reduction of fees under MCL 15.234 on the ground that disclosure of these records primarily benefits the general public rather than my commercial interest. The records concern a privately funded improvement to a City-owned facility and involve questions of public spending, private funding, public facilities, drinking water, and City operations that residents cannot fully evaluate from currently public sources.

If any portion of this request is denied, please cite the specific exemption claimed under MCL 15.243 for each withheld record and release all reasonably segregable non-exempt portions as required by MCL 15.244.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 5 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

John Doe

title: D.C. FOIA Request: Telecommunication Logs and Metadata for DISB Investigator Mark Pendleton (Jan 1, 2022 – Feb 28, 2022)

communications.subject: DC Freedom of Information Act Request: D.C. FOIA Request: Telecommunication Logs and Metadata for DISB Investigator Mark Pendleton (Jan 1, 2022 – Feb 28, 2022)
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the DC Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

To the Designated FOIA Officers for DISB and OCTO:

Under the District of Columbia Freedom of Information Act (D.C. Code §§ 2-531—539), I am formally requesting the production of the following specific server-level telecommunication records:

1. Inbound and Outbound Call Logs: Any and all telecommunication logs, billing records, or server-level metadata detailing inbound and outbound phone calls for the government-issued desk phone and/or government-issued mobile device assigned to DISB Investigator Mark Pendleton.
2. Timeframe: The strict chronological window of January 1, 2022, through February 28, 2022.
3. Required Data Fields: The requested logs must include, at a minimum: 
    * The date of each call.
    * The exact timestamp (start time) of each call.
    * The duration of each call.
    * The originating and terminating phone numbers (subject to valid redactions outlined below).

Clarifications to Prevent Improper Denial or Delay:

* This is NOT a request for an investigative case file. I am strictly requesting the server-level telecommunication metadata generated by the carrier/provider and maintained by the District of Columbia. Therefore, any claim that a specific "investigative file" was closed, deleted, or cannot be located is wholly irrelevant to the existence of these overarching municipal telecommunication billing records.
* Redaction Protocol: I acknowledge that under D.C. FOIA privacy exemptions, the agency may redact the specific, unlisted phone numbers of private citizens. However, the metadata itself (date, time, duration) cannot be redacted under this exemption. Furthermore, telephone numbers belonging to commercial entities, law firms, registered businesses, or other government agencies are not subject to personal privacy exemptions and must remain fully unredacted.

If this request is denied in whole or in part, please provide a detailed justification citing the specific statutory exemption under D.C. Code § 2-534 being invoked for each individual redaction or withheld record. 

Please provide these records in a digital spreadsheet or standard PDF format.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 15 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

FRED GAMBLE JR

communications.subject: Freedom of Information Act Request: FBI: Juris Upatnieks
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

I am requesting all records, files, dossiers, intelligence reports, correspondence, memoranda, and documentation maintaining a reference to or concerning the individual identified below:
Full Name: Juris Upatnieks
Date of Birth: May 7, 1936  
Place of Birth: Riga, Latvia  
Date of Death: May 17, 2026  
Key Historic Associations: Pioneer of laser holography, researcher at Willow Run Laboratories, the University of Michigan's Institute of Science and Technology, and the Environmental Research Institute of Michigan (ERIM).  

As the subject of this request is deceased as of May 17, 2026, privacy exemptions under 5 U.S.C. § 552(b)(7)(C) and (b)(6) regarding personal privacy are severely diminished. I have attached proof of death (obituary/public records documentation) to satisfy bureaucratic verification requirements. The death of Upatnieks was widely reported in the media:
https://eng.lsm.lv/article/society/education/26.05.2026-distinguished-scientist-juris-upatnieks-has-died.a648805/

Scope of Search & Explicit Directives:

Please conduct a thorough search of the Central Records System (CRS), including all main files and cross-references, electronic surveillance (ELSUR) indices, and any localized field office files (specifically looking at the Detroit and Cleveland Field Offices due to his historical residency and research hubs in Ann Arbor and Akron).
Furthermore, please execute the following parameters:

Please include a search of any records or documents understood to be, or colloquially referred to as "COINTELPRO records" under the bureau's custody that could be construed as responsive to a request for records related to Juris Upatnieks.

As you know, the Federal Bureau of Investigation is required to search the records stored at NARA’s Washington Records Center in Suitland, MD, but still technically under the FBI’s control. For more information see 36 CFR 1250.8. Please also conduct a physical search of records at NARA's Washington Records Center in Suitland for any records or documents related to Juris Upatnieks.

If the bureau cannot fulfill certain parts of this request or if certain portions must be redacted, please provide any reasonable segregable portion of a requested record after removing or redacting those portions claimed to be exempt, explain in writing the justification for redacting the record, indicate the extent of any redactions on the portion of the record which is made available or published and where technically feasible indicate on the redacted portion itself the specific exemption(s) claimed.

Thank for the work FBI does every day, and thank you for the bureau's commitment to transparency.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Robert Delaware

title: FBI: Merced civic leader Miller Alva Parker

communications.subject: Freedom of Information Act Request: FBI: Merced civic leader Miller Alva Parker
communications.communication:
To Whom It May Concern:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, I hereby request the following records:

I respectfully request copies of any and all records, files, memoranda, correspondence, reports, index references, administrative notations, surveillance records, investigative records, cross-references, or other materials maintained by the Federal Bureau of Investigation relating to:

Miller Alva Parker
Born: September 18, 1913 – Arkansas
Died: August 27, 1988 – Merced, California

Mr. Parker served in the United States Army during World War II from approximately 1942 to 1945. He was assigned to Ninth Headquarters in Europe and was discharged with the rank of Master Sergeant. Following World War II, he became a civic and business leader in Merced, California.

A copy of his 1940 draft registration card is attached to assist in identification and records searches. A story about this death ran in the Monday, August 29th, 1988 edition of the Merced Sun Star Newspaper. 

I request that the FBI conduct a comprehensive search of all indices and record systems reasonably likely to contain responsive records, including but not limited to:

Central Records System (CRS)
Electronic Surveillance (ELSUR) indices
Cross-reference (“see” and “see also”) files
Administrative files
Field office records
Security index records
Any retired paper records or microfilm holdings
Any files transferred to archival storage but remaining under FBI control

Please include a search of any records or documents understood to be, or colloquially referred to as "COINTELPRO records" under the bureau's custody that could be construed as responsive to a request for records related to Miller Alva Parker.

As you know, the Federal Bureau of Investigation is required to search the records stored at NARA’s Washington Records Center in Suitland, MD, but still technically under the FBI’s control. For more information see 36 CFR 1250.8.

Please also conduct a physical search of records at NARA's Washington Records Center in Suitland for any records or documents related to Miller Alva Parker.

In addition, I request that the FBI search for any “main files” as well as any references where Mr. Parker appears only incidentally, including name-reference entries, routing slips, communication logs, or enclosures to larger investigative files.

If any responsive records have been destroyed, transferred, or otherwise removed from FBI custody, I request any available documentation describing the disposition of those records, including destruction schedules, transfer documentation, or accession information.

Because the subject of this request is deceased, and died in 1988, privacy concerns should be substantially diminished. If proof of death is required, I am willing to provide supporting documentation.

If the agency cannot fulfill certain parts of this request or if certain portions must be redacted, please provide any reasonable segregable portion of a requested record after removing or redacting those portions claimed to be exempt, explain in writing the justification for redacting the record, indicate the extent of any redactions on the portion of the record which is made available or published and where technically feasible indicate on the redacted portion itself the specific exemption(s) claimed.

The requested documents will be made available to the general public, and this request is not being made for commercial purposes.

In the event that there are fees, I would be grateful if you would inform me of the total charges in advance of fulfilling my request. I would prefer the request filled electronically, by e-mail attachment if available or CD-ROM if not.

Thank you in advance for your anticipated cooperation in this matter. I look forward to receiving your response to this request within 20 business days, as the statute requires.

Sincerely,

Robert Delaware
